concerns.issue: Public humiliation of Ben (scene-8) could be distressing for players who have experienced similar situations, though the episode provides multiple response pathways including supportive interventions
concerns.location: Scene 8: 'Called On' - Ben stammering through presentation while Jordan's table snickers
concerns.recommendation: Consider adding a brief pre-scene content note: 'This scene includes mild peer mockery in a classroom setting. You'll have choices about how to respond.' This respects player agency while providing transparency

concerns.issue: Target age range listed as 11-14, but episode content and complexity are solidly middle-grade (11-13). The psychological sophistication of choice consequences and identity exploration may be slightly advanced for 11-year-olds, though not inappropriate
concerns.location: Overall episode design and thematic depth
concerns.recommendation: Consider clarifying target age as 11-14 with note that 11-year-olds may benefit from adult co-play or discussion. Content is not inappropriate for 11, just cognitively demanding

concerns.issue: Ben's character background includes parental separation and repeated school moves (trauma indicators), but these are mentioned only in character background, not explored in episode narrative. Risk of under-addressing his emotional needs
concerns.location: Ben's background section; not surfaced in episode dialogue
concerns.recommendation: Consider adding one brief line in Ben's dialogue (e.g., during project work) that hints at his background without requiring player to know it: 'I'm used to being the new person. You get good at reading rooms when you move a lot.' This makes his anxiety legible without heavy exposition

concerns.issue: The three branching paths (authentic, popularity, adaptive) are well-designed, but the 'popularity conformity' path (choice-5:b) doesn't explicitly surface consequences until later episodes. Players choosing that path may not recognize the emotional cost until future content
concerns.recommendation: In scene-11b (popularity path), add one additional line of internal monologue or observation that hints at cost: e.g., 'Walking off, you catch Ben's eyes for just a second — and something in his face closes.' This plants the seed of consequence within this episode without heavy-handedness

recommendations: Add optional pre-episode content note about peer mockery/social anxiety themes to support player choice-making

Consider a brief post-episode reflection prompt (outside the narrative) for players on the 'popularity' path: 'How did your choices feel? Would you do anything differently?' This encourages metacognition without judgment

Ensure that future episodes follow up on the branch outcomes consistently — players on the 'authentic' path should see Ben and Maya's trust deepen; players on 'popularity' path should see Ben's wariness persist
In future episodes, surface Ben's background (family instability, repeated moves) gradually through dialogue so players understand his anxiety is contextual, not inherent weakness

Consider adding one scene in a future episode where Jordan's own insecurity surfaces (his failing math, his fear of being 'forgettable'), creating opportunity for player to model the empathy they may have shown Ben

triggerWarnings.description: Peer mockery and social exclusion: Jordan makes a joke at Ben's expense in front of the class (scene-6); Ben stammers through a presentation while classmates snicker (scene-8). The episode provides player agency to intervene or not, but the bullying moment itself is present

Social anxiety and fear of rejection: Riley's internal monologue includes worry about 'being the new kid forever' and 'never having a place that feels solid'; Ben's character is defined by self-deprecation and social anxiety; Maya's background includes repeated school moves and friendship loss
triggerWarnings.location: Throughout episode, especially in internal monologues and character backgrounds

triggerWarnings.description: Parental separation and family instability: Riley's parents recently split time between two households; Ben's parents split two years ago; Maya's mother works night shifts, creating financial and emotional strain. These are mentioned in backgrounds but not dramatized in episode
triggerWarnings.location: Character background sections (not dramatized in episode narrative)

triggerWarnings.description: Social performance anxiety and fear of judgment: Multiple characters experience anxiety about social standing and peer perception. Ben's anxiety is particularly pronounced (stammering, apologizing preemptively, physical symptoms of nervousness)
triggerWarnings.location: Throughout episode, especially scenes 6-9 and character internal monologues

strengths: Excellent age-appropriate language and cognitive complexity: dialogue feels authentic to middle-grade voices without condescension; internal monologues are sophisticated but not overwrought

Strong social-emotional learning design: episode teaches empathy, perspective-taking, and consequence-awareness through player agency rather than explicit instruction. The three branching paths model different valid approaches to belonging without moralizing

Realistic portrayal of middle school social dynamics: the cafeteria seating choice, the hallway collision, the group project pairing, and the public humiliation moment are all authentic to 11-14 year old experience

Healthy representation of anxiety and social vulnerability: Ben's anxiety is portrayed as understandable and contextual (repeated moves, family instability), not as a character flaw. Multiple characters show realistic nervousness about social situations

Diverse, authentic character representation: Riley's multiracial identity, Jordan's split household, Ben's economic precarity, Maya's immigrant family background, and Ms. Alvarez's working-parent reality are all woven naturally into character design without tokenism

Player agency with emotional consequences: the choice structure genuinely matters. Choosing to support Ben vs. laugh along has different emotional payoffs, and the episode respects player intelligence by not making any choice 'obviously correct'
Inclusive pronoun handling: Riley's pronouns (she/they) are presented matter-of-factly as a player choice at game start, normalizing gender diversity without making it a plot point

Realistic portrayal of teacher limitations: Ms. Alvarez is warm and well-intentioned but overloaded and imperfect. She misses the moment of Ben's humiliation, which is realistic and creates space for player agency rather than relying on adult rescue

Hope and resilience present: all three branching paths end with some form of connection or possibility. Even the 'popularity' path doesn't end in tragedy — it ends in ambiguity, which is developmentally appropriate

Clear educational alignment: the episode directly teaches the stated goals (confidence as self-assertion, empathy through perspective-taking, adaptability as flexible authenticity, consequence-awareness)
improvements: Add optional content note before episode about peer mockery themes to support informed player choice

Enhance consequence visibility in 'popularity' path by adding one subtle line in scene-11b that hints at emotional cost (Ben's face closing, Maya's wariness) — this keeps the path valid while making consequences legible within the episode
Consider brief post-episode reflection prompt for players to metacognate on their choices without judgment
In future episodes, gradually surface Ben's background (family moves, instability) through dialogue so his anxiety becomes more legible as contextual rather than inherent
Ensure future episodes consistently follow branch outcomes — don't reset relationship dynamics between episodes
Consider adding one moment in a future episode where Jordan's own insecurity/academic struggle surfaces, creating opportunity for player empathy to extend to him
